info.structure: Part 21: Chapter 2, Section 9. The Sevenfold Sāman Meditation on the Sun (Āditye Saptavidha Sāmopāsanam). This section applies the newly introduced sevenfold framework to the daily solar cycle, mapping the changing states of daylight from sunrise to midnight onto the liturgical steps of the chant.
info.format: Devanagari, IAST, English translation, and philosophical commentaries.

sections.title: The Sevenfold Sāman Meditation on the Sun

verses.devanagari: अथ खल्वमुमादित्यं सप्तविधं सामोपासीत सर्वदा समस्तेन समस्तन्मां प्रति मां प्रतीति सर्वेण समः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha khalvamumādityaṃ saptavidhaṃ sāmopāseeta sarvadā samastena samastanmāṃ prati māṃ pratīti sarveṇa samaḥ || 1 ||
verses.english_translation: Next, one should meditate on the sun as the sevenfold Sāman. The sun is always the same (Sama) to everyone, because everyone thinks: 'He faces me, he faces me!' Thus, being equal to all, the sun is the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara highlights a beautiful psychological and cosmic observation here. The word *Sāman* is etymologically tied to *Sama* (equality or sameness). The sun represents the ultimate visible emblem of this equality. No matter where a person stands on earth, the sun appears to look directly at them (*māṃ prati*). It does not discriminate based on geography, morality, or status. Because of this absolute, unbroken impartiality, the sun is identical to the universal harmony of the Sāman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that this equality is an essential attribute of the Supreme Lord (*Sambhava*). The sun, as the eye of the cosmic body of God, shines on all souls with equal love and opportunity. This meditation breaks down the narrow, competitive ego of the seeker, reminding them that divine grace is infinitely distributed and accessible to everyone.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the indwelling deity of the sun (*Sūrya-Nārāyaṇa*) acts with perfect justice and impartiality under the direct orders of Lord Viṣṇu. While individual souls vary in their capacity to receive light due to their own past actions (*karma*), the sun itself offers its vitalizing energy equally to the entire dependent universe.

verses.devanagari: तस्मिन्निमानि सर्वाणि भूतान्यन्वायत्तानीति विद्यात्तस्य यत्पुरोदयात्सा हिंकारस्तदस्य पशवोऽन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते हिं कुर्वन्ति हिंकारभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: tasminnimāni sarvāṇi bhūtānyanvāyattānīti vidyāttasya yatpurodayātsā hiṃkārastodasya paśavo'nvāyattāstasmātte hiṃ kurvanti hiṃkārabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ || 2 ||
verses.english_translation: One should know that all living beings depend on the sun. That form of the sun which appears before its actual sunrise is the Hiṃkāra. On this stage, the animals are dependent; therefore, animals make the 'Hiṃ' sound at dawn, for they share in this initial part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the first of the seven solar stages: the pre-dawn glow (*purodayāt*). This dim, initial twilight is mapped to *Hiṃkāra*. The text notes that domestic animals and livestock are deeply sensitive to this specific frequency of morning energy. They awake and make guttural noises (*hiṃ-kurvanti*). By meditating on this connection, the chanter recognizes that animal behavior is naturally hardwired into the cosmic choir of the Sāman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school points out that the morning awakening of animals demonstrates how physical nature responds instinctively to the first stirrings of divine light. This stage teaches the human seeker to wake up early and prepare their mind, ensuring they do not miss the initial daily downpouring of spiritual vitality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the minor deities ruling the animal kingdom activate the biological clocks of creatures during pre-dawn twilight. This structural dependency proves that animal life is not an isolated mechanical process but is explicitly synchronized with the solar administration designed by the Lord.

verses.devanagari: अथ यत्प्रथमोदिते स प्रस्तावस्तदस्य मनुष्या अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते प्रकटाः प्रशंसाकामाः प्रस्तावभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: atha yatprathamodite sa prastāvastodasya manuṣyā anvāyattāstasmātte prakaṭāḥ praśaṃsākāmāḥ prastāvabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ || 3 ||
verses.english_translation: The form of the sun when it first rises is the Prastāva. On this stage, humans are dependent; therefore, humans desire advancement, public presentation, and praise, for they share in this introductory praise part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the second stage: the actual moment of sunrise (*prathamodite*), mapped to *Prastāva* (the introductory praise). This is the sphere of human dependency. As the sun rises, human society springs into action, looking for growth, public activity (*prakaṭāḥ*), and social validation or praise (*praśaṃsā-kāmāḥ*). The prefix *Pra-* links the psychological drives of humanity directly to the structural properties of the *Prastāva* chant.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that human ambition and the urge to perform good deeds are activated by the rising sun. The text highlights this drive to remind seekers that their daily work and desire for excellence should be offered up as an intentional praise-offering (*prastāva*) to the indwelling Divine Lord rather than being wasted on pure ego inflation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the rising sun stimulates the intellectual and physical faculties of human beings. Humans are uniquely endowed with the capacity to praise the Lord. Recognizing this connection helps the devotee understand that their daily duties and social responsibilities are structural assignments given by the solar administration.

verses.devanagari: अथ यत्संगववेलायां सा आदिस्तदस्य वयांस्यन्वायत्तानि तस्मात्तान्यन्तरिक्षेऽनारम्भणान्यात्मानमुत्पतन्त्युपादायैतस्य साम्नः ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: atha yatsaṃgavavelāyāṃ sā ādistodasya vayāṃsyanvāyattāni tasmāttānyantarikṣe'nārambhaṇānyātmānamutpatantyupādāyaitasya sāmnaḥ || 4 ||
verses.english_translation: The form of the sun at the hour of cow-gathering (mid-morning) is the Ādi. On this stage, the birds are dependent; therefore, they fly about in the intermediate space without any support, holding themselves up, for they share in this initial part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara clarifies *saṃgava-velā*, the mid-morning hour when cows are gathered together to be milked or driven to pasture. This time corresponds to the *Ādi* syllable (typically *Om*). Birds (*vayāṃsi*) are the creatures dependent on this specific solar hour. Sustained by the warming air currents of mid-morning, they launch themselves into the trackless sky, flying gracefully without physical pillars or support (*anārambhaṇāni*). They match the soaring, independent quality of the sacred sound *Om*.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school interprets the flight of the birds as a metaphor for spiritual surrender. Just as a bird trusts the invisible air of mid-morning to hold it aloft without any solid ground beneath its feet, the soul must trust the all-pervading grace of the Lord (*Ādi* / *Om*) to sustain them in the spiritual sky.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the mid-morning solar rays provide the exact thermodynamic conditions that allow birds to thrive and fly. This demonstrates that even the movements of wild animals in the sky are meticulously supported by the cosmic infrastructure managed by Viṣṇu.

verses.devanagari: अथ यन्मध्यंदिने स उद्गीथस्तदस्य देवा अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते प्राजापत्यानां दायादानां श्रेष्ठा उद्गीथभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha yanmadhyaṃdine sa udgīthastodasya devā anvāyattāstasmātte prājāpatyānāṃ dāyādānāṃ śreṣṭhā udgīthabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ || 5 ||
verses.english_translation: The form of the sun at exact midday is the Udgītha. On this stage, the gods (Devas) are dependent; therefore, they are the most excellent among the offspring of Prajāpati, for they share in this supreme part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the apex of the solar journey: noon (*madhyaṃdine*), which is mapped to the *Udgītha*, the highest and most glorious portion of the chant. This peak energy belongs to the gods (*devas*). Because they are synchronized with the sun at its brightest, most shadowless point, the gods possess radiant bodies and represent the most excellent (*śreṣṭhāḥ*) branch of the creation of Prajāpati.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that midday represents the full manifestation of light and knowledge, entirely free from the shadows of ignorance. The gods thrive in this clarity. Meditating on this peak teaches the seeker to aspire for midday clarity in their own consciousness, driving away the dark shadows of doubt and attachment.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that at noon, the solar administrative deities operate at maximum strength. The excellence of the gods over demons and humans is due to their pristine alignment with this peak solar force, showing that spiritual hierarchy in the universe is determined by proximity to divine light.

verses.devanagari: अथ यदपराह्णे स प्रतिहारस्तदस्य गर्भा अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते प्रतिहृता नावपद्यन्ते प्रतिहारभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadapārāhṇe sa pratihārastodasya garbhā anvāyattāstasmātte pratihṛtā nāvapadyante pratihārabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ || 6 ||
verses.english_translation: The form of the sun in the afternoon is the Pratihāra. On this stage, embryos (fetuses in the womb) are dependent; therefore, they are held back and secured in the womb without falling out, for they share in this protective, holding part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara delivers a fascinating medical and esoteric insight on the afternoon sun (*aparāhṇe*), mapping it to the *Pratihāra* (which literally means checking, holding back, or defending). The entities dependent on this phase are embryos (*garbhāḥ*). The closing, cooling energy of the afternoon matches the biological mechanism that keeps a fetus safely contained and held up within the mother's womb, preventing miscarriage (*nāvapadyante*). This links cosmic cooling to biological stabilization.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights the deep, protective aspect of this verse. The Lord does not just create; He holds and preserves. By identifying the afternoon sun with the retention of life in the womb, the text reminds the seeker that they are constantly held, sheltered, and nurtured by the divine motherly grace of God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the afternoon solar energies are governed by deities specializing in preservation and structural stability. The retention of life in the womb during this shifting period of the day is a testament to the intricate protective laws established by the independent will of Viṣṇu.

verses.devanagari: अथ यदस्तमिते स उपद्रवस्तदस्य आरण्या अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते पुरुषं दृष्ट्वा कक्षं श्वभ्रभित्यूपद्रवन्त्युपद्रवभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadastamite sa upadravastodasya āraṇyā anvāyattāstasmātte puruṣaṃ dṛṣṭvā kakṣaṃ śvabhramityūpadravantyupadravabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ || 7 ||
verses.english_translation: The form of the sun immediately after sunset is the Upadrava. On this stage, the wild animals of the forest are dependent; therefore, when they see a human, they run away to their dens or thickets, for they share in this fleeing part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the sixth stage: the immediate aftermath of sunset (*astamite*), mapped to the *Upadrava* (which means running away, escaping, or a secondary phase). The wild animals of the forest (*āraṇyāḥ*) depend on this hour. As darkness falls, their defensive instincts sharpen; if they spot a human, they bolt (*upadravanti*) into caves or thickets. The wordplay connects the flight of the wild beasts directly to the musical *Upadrava* phase.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that sunset marks the transition where wild creatures reclaim their domain. Their instinct to flee humans is a protective boundary set by the Lord. This stage teaches the seeker that when the dark periods of life arrive, one must withdraw from outer social distractions and retreat into the inner sanctuary of the heart.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the sunset triggers the nocturnal and defensive operations of forest life. The instinctual fear and retreat of wild animals show that even the emotions of fear and self-preservation are regulated by cosmic cycles under divine governance.

verses.devanagari: अथ यद्रहोऽस्तमिते तन्निधनं तदस्य पितरोऽन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्तान्निदधति निधनभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्न एवं खल्वमुमादित्यं सप्तविधं सामोपास्ते ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadraho'stamite tannidhanaṃ tadasya pitaro'nvāyattāstasmāttānnidadhati nidhanabhājino hyetasya sāmna evaṃ khalvamumādityaṃ saptavidhaṃ sāmopāste || 8 ||
verses.english_translation: The form of the sun when it is completely hidden (midnight/deep night) is the finale (Nidhana). On this stage, the ancestors (Pitris) are dependent; therefore, people deposit funeral offerings for them or lay them down, for they share in this final resting part of the Sāman. Thus, verily, one meditates on the sun as the sevenfold Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara concludes this magnificent section with the status of the sun when it is entirely out of sight (*raho'stamite*), meaning midnight or the deep unmanifest dark. This corresponds to the *Nidhana* (the final dissolution or storage house). The ancestors (*pitaraḥ*) depend on this phase, as the unmanifest night connects our world with the realm of the departed. The word *Nidhana* matches *nidadhati* (the ritual act of placing down or depositing offerings for the dead). This completes the sevenfold solar journey, proving that light and dark, life and death, are bound within a single cosmic song.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that *Nidhana* represents the ultimate rest of the soul. The ancestors residing in the higher spiritual planes find their peace in the deep unmanifest phase of the divine cycle. It reassures the seeker that death is not absolute destruction, but a temporary resting phase inside the infinite memory of God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the deep night is administered by deities ruling over sleep, rest, and the realms of the Pitris. Depositing pindas or memory offerings during specific cycles connects the living with the departed under the single, overarching law of Lord Viṣṇu, who preserves the souls of the ancestors just as carefully as He maintains the living.
